coredata.dc:title: A sensitive dual mode turn-on fluorescence and colorimetric nanosensor for ultrasensitive detection of trace amount of gluten proteins in bread products based on crystalline nano cellulose and gold nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Sadat Mousavizadeh, Fatemeh
Sarlak, Nahid
coredata.dc:description: In this work, Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) encapsulated in the surface of crystalline nano cellulose grafted poly citric acid (CNC-g-PCA) and CNC-g-PCA/Au nanocomposite were synthesized successfully that exhibited stable and intense fluorescence property in aqueous buffer. A dual-mode nanosensor is reported with both colorimetric and fluorimetric readout based on citrate-protected AuNPs for discriminative detection of gluten proteins. The proposed sensing system consists of AuNPs and fluorescent CNCs, where CNCs function as a fluorimetric reporter and AuNPs serve a dual function as a colorimetric reporter and fluorescence quencher. The mechanism of the reported dual-mode nanosensor is based on two distance-dependent phenomena, the color change of AuNPs and FRET. The presence of gluten proteins can reverse the process by enlarging the inter-particle distance between AuNPs and CNCs and recovering the fluorescence emission of CNC. The linear range was 0.05 to 0.40 μgmL−1 for UV–vis spectroscopy and 0.017 to 0.298 μgmL−1 for fluorescence spectroscopy, The limit of detection was 4.43 ± 0.019 ngmL−1 for UV–vis spectroscopy and 3.13 ± 0.033 ngmL−1 for fluorescence spectroscopy (n = 6). The fabricated nanosensor was applied to the gluten analysis in gluten-free bread successfully.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gluten detection
Gold nanoparticles
Crystalline nano cellulose
Turn-on fluorescence
Fluorescence gluten nanosensor
Colorimetric gluten nanosensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of gluten in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Lorestan University high education Research Council

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Lorestan University high education Research Council for financial support of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of CNC, CNC-g-PCA and CNC-g-PCA/Au nanocomposite. (B)The Uv–Vis absorption spectra of CNC-g-PCA and CNC-g-PCA/Au. The characteristic surface plasmon band at 530 nm and ruby red color of gold nanoparticles in CNC-g-PCA/Au. And the excitation spectrum at 310 nm and emission spectrum 447 nm of CNC-g-PCA/Au.
SEM images of CNCs (a-b) and the CNC-g-PCA/Au nanocomposites (c-d).

(A) photographic images of changing color from red to light purple resulted from the addition of gluten to CNC-g-PCA/Au solution 0 to 0.50 μgmL−1 from left to right. (B) Turning on fluorescence photographic images of CNC-g-PCA/Au nanocomposite in presence of 0.00 to 6.87 μgmL−1 gluten from left to right (C) Changing Uv–Vis spectra of CNC-g-PCA/Au suspension in the presence of gluten. (D) Uv–Vis spectra of CNC-g-PCA/Au suspension in the presence of different gluten concentrations 0 to 0.60 μgmL−1. (E) Fluorescence spectra of CNC-g-PCA/Au suspension in the presence of different gluten concentrations 0 to 0.373 μgmL−1.
SEM images of CNC-g-PCA/Au suspension in the presence of gluten (a-d). 2D (e and f) and 3D (g and h) AFM images of CNC-g-PCA/Au suspension in the presence of gluten.
(A) The absorption ratio A720/A530 of CNC-g-PCA/Au in different pH media. (B) Response time of CNCN-g-PCA/Au in the presence of gluten.
(A) Calibration curve of A530/A720 vs gluten concentration in the CNC-g-PCA/Au suspension. (B) Calibration curve of fluorescence intensity vs gluten concentration in the CNC-g-PCA/Au suspension.
Schematic of the reaction of gluten proteins and CNC-g-PCA/Au.
Study of the interfering influence on the detection of 0.150 μgmL−1 gluten in pH 5 at 1 min. The concentrations of organic species were 7.50 μgmL−1 and inorganic ions were 15.00 μgmL−1, respectively.

row.entry: Na+, NH4 +, Cl-, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, PO4 3-, NO3 –, SO4 2-

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Figure of merit for UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy methods.

Analytical results of the determination of spiked gluten proteins in gluten-free bread samples with 2 different concentrations (1.0 and 2.0 µgmL−1 for UV–vis, 1.5 and 3.0 µgmL−1 for fluorescence) by the designed dual-mode nanosensor.

entry.bold: “Celino Kalleh” gluten free white toast bread

head.ce:title: A sensitive dual mode turn-on fluorescence and colorimetric nanosensor for ultrasensitive detection of trace amount of gluten proteins in bread products based on crystalline nano cellulose and gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Lorestan University, Khorramabad, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Lorestan University, Khorramabad, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) encapsulated in the surface of crystalline nano cellulose grafted poly citric acid (CNC-g-PCA) and CNC-g-PCA/Au nanocomposite were synthesized successfully that exhibited stable and intense fluorescence property in aqueous buffer. A dual-mode nanosensor is reported with both colorimetric and fluorimetric readout based on citrate-protected AuNPs for discriminative detection of gluten proteins. The proposed sensing system consists of AuNPs and fluorescent CNCs, where CNCs function as a fluorimetric reporter and AuNPs serve a dual function as a colorimetric reporter and fluorescence quencher. The mechanism of the reported dual-mode nanosensor is based on two distance-dependent phenomena, the color change of AuNPs and FRET. The presence of gluten proteins can reverse the process by enlarging the inter-particle distance between AuNPs and CNCs and recovering the fluorescence emission of CNC. The linear range was 0.05 to 0.40 μgmL−1 for UV–vis spectroscopy and 0.017 to 0.298 μgmL−1 for fluorescence spectroscopy, The limit of detection was 4.43 ± 0.019 ngmL−1 for UV–vis spectroscopy and 3.13 ± 0.033 ngmL−1 for fluorescence spectroscopy (n = 6). The fabricated nanosensor was applied to the gluten analysis in gluten-free bread successfully.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Proteins are basis biomaterials that play significant roles in the life activities [1]. The storage proteins of the wheat seed that called glutens are able to form a unique viscoelastic network. The properties of gluten make it useful in the process of making food products such as bread, pasta and semolina, and more recently bio packaging materials [2]. The gluten forming storage proteins are further composed of two fractions- the alcohol soluble gliadins and the alcohol insoluble glutenins. Gliadin is responsible for gluten intolerance, bakers’ asthma, and wheat-dependent exercise-induced anaphylaxis and is known as a major allergen [3,4]. The most classic immune-mediated disorder is celiac disease affecting almost 1 % of the population in the world and related to gluten ingestion [5]. To date, the only treatment is a strict gluten-free diet that resolves the symptoms of patients influenced by these disorders. “Gluten-free” foods define as those having 20 mgkg−1 or less amount of gluten. Therefore, development of a highly sensitive, cost-effective and small-sized nanosensors to determine the storage of gluten proteins in foods is necessary [6].

Metal nanoparticles especially gold nanoparticles are considered as the desired candidates for fabricating both chemical sensors and biosensors [7–14]. AuNPs due to their chemical, physical such as high surface-to-volume ratio, electrical and fascinating optical properties offer multiple platforms for the detection of chemical analytes, disease agents and allergens with a series of processes, including spectroscopic (i.e. Surface Enhanced Raman Scattering, SERS) [15,16], colorimetric[17–19], fluorimetric [20–23] and electrochemical methods [24–25]. The optical properties of AuNPs greatly depend on their morphological and physiological characteristics i.e. their size, shape and aggregation state, which can be fine-tuned by choosing the appropriate synthesis and stabilization agent [26]. In the most of cases, the AuNPs nanosensors can be achieved by evaluating the changes in the color Due to aggregation/dissociation processes, which can be red to purple, to blue and almost black [27]. This is the basis of colorimetric detection, wherein binding of the target analyte to AuNPs surface results in aggregation, ultimately leading to change in color, related to broadening and shifting of Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) peak [28].

The most important application of AuNPs is colorimetric and fluorescence sensing, which provides high sensitivities and low detection limits [26]. In recent years, gold nanoparticles were synthesized via different methods and the primary substance for detection of proteins. A sensitive “turn-on” sensor based on Ce-MOF and AuNPs was fabricated for the detection of Glutathione (GSH). The formation Au-S bond between GSH and AuNPs prevents the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between Ce-MOF and AuNPs [29]. In another try for Glutathione study, a sensitive, label-free and dual-mode nanosensor was fabricated based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and gold nanoparticles for discriminative Glutathione detection. The mechanism of the nanosensor was based on two distance-dependent phenomena, the color change of AuNPs and FRET. This nanosensor had a good function in complicated biological fluids [30]. In another study, a nanosensor was developed to discriminate cysteine (Cys) from homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH) with multiple signals: colorimetric, photoluminescence (PL) and up-conversional photoluminescence (UCP). The nanosensor (NCdots/AuNPs) was constructed by N-doped carbon dots (NC-dots) and Au nanoparticles by assembling NC-dots “shell” on AuNPs and showed the obvious different responses to Cys, Hcy, and GSH with colorimetric, PL and UCP signals [10]. In another work, the immunological method was demonstrated for protein detection based on the aggregation of antibody-functionalized gold nanoparticles. The effect of the size of the gold nanoparticles on the sensitivity of the nanosensor was studied in the range from 20 to 60 nm particles; the larger particles shown here the highest relative changes in hydrodynamic diameter in dynamic light scattering (DLS) and UV/Vis spectroscopy [31].

In this paper, we have fabricated a simple and fast dual-mode nanosensor with colorimetric and fluorimetric (‘turn on’ fluorescence change) to determine gluten directly. Colloidal gold nanoparticles are generally unstable and tend to agglomerate in solution due to the low repulsive forces between particles, leading to the formation of large particles with reduced catalytic activity. Therefore, they are usually encapsulated in polymers or supported by more colloidal stable materials (e.g. metal/silica oxides, carbon nano materials) [32]. The encapsulation of AuNPs on the surface of modified crystalline nano cellulose with poly citric acid (CNC-g-PCA/Au) provides a new approach to fabricate nanosensor for detection of gluten proteins. Dispersed AuNPs are synthesized in an eco-friendly way, which is different than previous approaches and without harsh reduction reagents. Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) are used to figure out the interaction between CNC-g-PCA/Au and gluten. When CNC-g-PCA/Au exposure to gluten, AuNPs aggregate and thus enlarging the inter-particle distance between AuNPs and CNCs. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of the CNC-g-PCA/Au was recovered and the amount of it was strongly dependent on the amount of added gluten. Scheme 1 illustrates the assay procedure for the colorimetric detection of the gluten.

The gluten powder was used to prepare a stock gluten solution. it was prepared from Ard Azin, Isfahan, Iran. All of the solvents (ammonia, ethanol, tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethylenediamine), tetrachloroauric (III) acid trihydrate (HAuCl4 0.3 H2O) 99.5 %, sodium chloride, Citric Acid-Monohydrate (code: 100242) and other materials were obtained from Merck company.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Mira3 Tescan, Czech Republic), equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, Mira3 Te scan, Czech Republic), was used to observe nanocomposite and nanosensor morphologies. Bio Atomic Force Microscope (AFM, Ara-research, Iran) was used to investigate the topography and morphology of the samples as well as the roughness of the samples. Infrared spectrums were obtained from Shimadzu FT-IR 160 spectrophotometer (Japan) using KBr pellets. A Shimadzu Uv–Visible spectrophotometer (model UV-1800) with 1-cm glass cells was used for recording the absorption spectra measurements. Fluorescence spectra were taken on Agilent Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (USA) Cary-Eclipse model. The excitation and emission slit widths both were set at 5 and 10 nm.

This method consists of two steps, at first, nano cellulose crystalline is synthesized by acid hydrolysis method. Then the poly citric acid chain attached to functional groups on CNC by covalent bonding.

In this method, 4 g cotton was cut into smaller pieces; then 2 % NaOH solution to purify cotton was slowly added to it at room temperature and vigorously stirred for 12 h. The NaOH solution was removed from the activated cotton with filter paper and was rinsed with deionized water to remove the remaining NaOH to pH 7. Then, for acid hydrolysis, 35 mL of 65 % sulfuric acid was gradually added to it at 45 °C and stirred with a magnetic stirrer. After 120 min, nanocellulose was prepared. The suspension was subject to centrifugation (6000 rpm for 5 min per time) and was diluted 10 times with deionized water till neutrality. The turbid supernatant was ultrasonicated for 5 h. The prepared crystalline nanocellulose were freeze-dried.

The synthesized CNC was polymerized with poly citric acid according to the reported procedure in the literature [33]. In brief, the 0.050 g of CNC were transferred to a polymerization ampule, 2.5 g of citric acid was added, and were sealed under vacuum. The mixture was heated from 120° C to 160° C while stirring for 3 h. The obtained nanocomposite (CNC-g-PCA) was dissolved in THF, then filtered, and was allowed to evaporate the solvent at room temperature.

CNC-g-PCA/Au nanocomposite was synthesized via a chemical reduction and sonochemical approach in this study. 1:0.2 solution of CNC-g-PCA and HAuCl4 nanocomposites was prepared and pH was adjusted to 5. The solution was then stirred for 4 h at room temperature till ruby red color.

The powder contained 79 % proteins, including 44.6 % gliadins and 55.4 % glutenins. Defatted gluten powder was prepared by stirring 50 g gluten powder with 250 mL dimethyl chloride for 30 min at room temperature. The grout was then filtered, and the residue solvent was evaporated under the frame hood. 37.5 g of powder was dispersed in 150 mL of 0.4 M NaCl solution and was shaken for 30 min at room temperature, to remove starch and the flour metabolic proteins, which are not contained in the gluten structure. This process was repeated till the not change the color of Potassium Iodide. Then it was centrifuged for 10 min at 6000 rpm at 20 °C. The pellet was manually re-suspended three times in 200 mL of a 55 % ethanol–water solution to solute gluten proteins. The dispersed solution was stirred and centrifuged. The 55 % ethanol–water solution contains both gliadins and glutenins. Gliadins and glutenins are separated by phase separation at low temperatures. A solution enriched in gliadin (up to 82 %) was achieved from the solution after one night of resting at 4 ˚C and centrifugation at 2 ˚C for 10 min at 20000 rpm. The sediment was primarily involved in glutenin and was discarded. The ethanol–water solution enriched in gliadin was freeze-dried and the powder was stored in the refrigerator. As regards, extraction of gluten proteins was carried out with ethanol–water 55 % from wheat gluten powder which its fat, starch, and metabolic proteins have been removed and also the glutenins were separated by phase separation at low temperatures. The residual solid contains more than 90 % gliadins. The gluten stock solution was obtained using powder in a 55 % ethanol–water solvent [34].

Different concentrations of gluten were mixed with CNC-g-PCA/Au solution in a quartz cell to detect gluten proteins. SEM and AFM images, Uv–Vis and fluorescence spectra were taken after adding gluten solutions to the dispersed AuNPs for 1 min.

FTIR Spectroscopy: The FT-IR spectra of CNC, CNC-g-PCA and CNC-g-PCA/Au nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 1 A. The FT-IR spectrum of CNC shows that the characteristic peak of the alcoholic OH group appears at 3441 cm−1. The peak of 2902 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetrically stretching vibration of the CH aliphatic bond. Another peak at 1369 cm−1 is related to asymmetric stretching of the CH bond. The peak 1093 cm−1 indicates the COC cellulose pyranose ring and the feature peaks between 1000 and 1170 cm−1 are due to the weak CO stretching vibration. The peaks at 667 cm−1 was related to the Iβ phase of cellulose [35,36]. In the FT-IR spectrum of CNC-g-PCA, the band at 3400 cm−1 corresponds to CO-H and OH bodes and esterification of the carboxylic acid groups of citric acid. The band at 2981 cm−1 is related to –CH2- groups of citric acid and the band at 1723 cm−1 is due to the ester group because of the polymerized citric acid chains. The two bands at 1404 and 1193 cm−1, both indicate the existence of carbonyl groups -CO, -CC and -CO bonds, respectively. The epoxy adsorption band is being removed from here due to the polymerization procedures [33,37]. The FT-IR CNC-g-PCA/Au spectrum shows a decrease in intensity at the peaks of the acid functional groups due to the encapsulation of gold nanoparticles in the citric acid polymer chain, which shows the formation of Au-O bonds. Also, the peaks at 1193 and 1404 cm−1 shift to 1226 and 1419 cm−1 due to the encapsulation of gold nanoparticles [38].

SEM Imaging: Fig. 2 shows the morphology of the CNC and CNC-g-PCA/Au by FE-SEM Analysis. Morphological analysis in Fig. 2(a-b) show the Needle-like structure of CNC and Fig. 2(c-d) show encapsulated gold nanoparticles as bright spots in the shell of poly citric acid.

Fig. 2B displays the UV–vis spectra of CNC-g-PCA and CNC-g-PCA/Au. The characteristic surface plasmon resonance band of gold nanoparticles appeared at 530 nm. Also, the ruby red color of encapsulated gold nanoparticles indicates their successful synthesis with less than 40 nm diameters. When the CNC-g-PCA excites at 310 nm, emits an intense fluorescence at 447 nm. The characteristic SPR peak of gold nanoparticles at 522 nm overlaps with the CNC emission spectrum at 447 nm (Fig. 2B).

The suggested nanosensor includes AuNPs and fluorescent CNC, where CNC functions as a fluorimetric reporter and AuNPs perform a dual role as a colorimetric reporter and fluorescence quencher. The mechanism of the reported dual-mode nanosensor is based on two distance-dependent phenomena, the color change of AuNPs and FRET. AuNPs possess distance-dependent optical properties and are known to have a superior quenching efficiency in a broad range of wavelengths. Scheme 1, shows the concept of this proposed procedure, the schematic of the formation of reduced gold nanoparticles by polymeric citric acid and future reaction with gluten proteins. First, Au+3 ions (HAuCl4) reduce to produce colloidal Au0 by transferring electrons from the polymeric shell of CNC-g-PCA, then, nucleate and grow to form gold nanoparticles, which are encapsulated and stabilized by the citric acid polymeric shell [39]. The absorption at 530 nm in Fig. 2A is related to the surface plasmon resonance of gold nanoparticles.

The aggregation of nanocomposites exposed to gluten can be visualized naked eye qualitatively (Fig. 3 A) and quantitated by fluorescence and Uv–Vis spectroscopy. CNC-g-PCA/Au are dispersed in the solution. The repulsion of negative ions on the surface of CNC nanocomposite prevent the aggregation of AuNPs in the solution. When gluten solutions with different concentrations are added to the dispersed AuNPs, the color of the nanocomposite changes from red to purple and light purple (Fig. 3A) [30,40]. Carboxylate groups on the surface of CNC-g-PCA/Au are good adsorbents for biomolecules such as proteins. The adsorption is occurred through electrostatic interaction between the surface-terminated anionic groups –COO− on the nanocomposites and the amino groups –NH3 + with the positive charge of the gluten proteins. Besides the electrostatic interaction, ionic/hydrogen bonding between –NH3 + and –COO− on the functionalized surface is also possible [30,40]. As shown in Fig. 3C, with increasing gluten concentration, the intensity of the nanocomposite absorption at 530 nm decreases, while a new band appears at 720 nm. Then, with more concentration of gluten in the solution (as shown in Fig. 3A), the colloidal nanoparticles aggregate and precipitate in the cell; therefore, the absorption of the nanocomposite decrease at both 720 nm and 530 nm. Fig. 3B, 3D and 3E illustrate the recovery of fluorescence of CNC-g-PCA/Au nanocomposites, the decrease in UV–vis absorption and the increase in fluorescence of the probe after reaction with gluten, respectively [21,30,41–43]. When AuNPs are encapsulated in the polymeric shell of CNC-g-PCA, the fluorescent emission of CNCs substantially decreases due to the energy transfer from CNC to AuNPs within close proximity, while gluten proteins can reverse the process by enlarging the inter-particle distance between AuNPs and CNC. As expected, AuNPs did quench the CNC fluorescence, and the presence of gluten resulted in the recovery of strong fluorescent emission of CNC (Fig. 3B) [21,30].

Morphology monitoring is the most direct way to evaluate the analytes and nanostructures reaction. The CNC-g-PCA/Au and gluten reaction was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). Fig. 4 (a-d) shows the SEM images of the aggregated AuNPs in the CNC-g-PCA/Au nanocomposites after exposure to gluten. Fig. 4 (e-h) illustrate 2D and 3D topographic images of nanocomposites before and after exposure to gluten. The bright spots define encapsulated gold nanoparticles, which are dispersed before reaction. The absence of dispersed gold nanoparticles in Fig. 4f is due to the aggregation of gold nanoparticles. The SEM and AFM results are complete and confirm the sensing mechanism.

Effect of pH: The pH of media is an important factor in the aggregation of gold nanoparticles. To monitor the effect of the pH of the medium, the absorption measurements were carried out at pH values of 3 to 9. The results showed that almost the absorption ratio A720/A530 increased with increasing pH value as Fig. 5 A, because of aggregation of gold nanoparticles in alkali pH. Thus, the pH 5 was chosen as optimum value.

Response time: The kinetics of the gluten assay by CNC-g-PCA/Au were investigated by monitoring the fluorescence intensity vs time. The results are shown in Fig. 5B. The fluorescence intensity of CNC-g-PCA/Au solutions with 0.166 µgmL−1 gluten proteins increased within 1 min and then remained constant when elongated the time. Thus, all measurements were recorded at 1 min.

Study of the interfering influence: To evaluate the selectivity of this assay, the effect of some potential interference species such as glucose, starch, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, NH4 +, PO4 3-, K+, Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2- and citrate, on the detection of 0.150 μgml−1 gluten. The concentration was 7.5 μgml−1 for organic species and 15 μgml−1 for inorganic ions, which was 50 and 100 times gluten concentration. The tolerance was defined as the concentration of the added ion causing a relative error of less than 5 %. The results are given in Table 1 that shows the method has good specificity and selectivity.

The calibration curve for gluten determination was constructed by plotting absorbance ratio A720/A530 changes and Fluorescence as a function of the gluten concentration. As illustrated in Fig. 6 A and B, the graphs display a linear range of gluten concentrations from 0.05 to 0.40 μgmL−1 for UV–vis spectroscopy and 0.017 to 0.298 μgmL−1 for fluorescence spectroscopy at pH 5 and 1 min. The regression equation is y = 1.4765x − 0.0082 with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9991 for UV–vis spectroscopy and y = 478.7x + 223.92 with R2 = 0.9986 for fluorescence spectroscopy. The limit of detection value was 4.43 ± 0.019 ngmL−1 for UV–vis spectroscopy and 3.13 ± 0.033 ngmL−1 for fluorescence spectroscopy, which was calculated using three times the blank standard deviation (3Sb), divided by the slope (m) of the calibration curve (3Sb/m). The characteristics of the calibration curve and validation parameters are presented in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of gluten in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical efficiency of the developed biosensor was evaluated to determine gluten in two gluten-free bread from “Kaleh Company”. The extraction of gluten from heat-treated food is complicated issues in the food industry that has developed based on several extraction solutions. Most of these protocols used alcohol-water solutions to extract gluten from the food sample treated with 0.5 M NaCl to remove albumins and globulins [44,45]. Therefore, the treated gluten-free breads were first extracted with 55 % ethanol and determined with nanoprobe, then spiked with highly concentrated gluten in 55 % ethanol. The results are shown in Table 3 .

The addition standard recovery method was used for developed nanoprobe. For this purpose, a certain amount of gluten standard solution (1.0 and 2.0 µgmL−1 for UV–vis, 1.5 and 3.0 µgmL−1 for fluorescence) was added to “gluten-free” real samples, the measurements were repeated three times. The results showed the excellent reliability and reproducibility of the method for determining the gluten in bread samples.

A dual-mode nanosensor was developed with both UV–vis and fluorescence readout for the quantitative detection of gluten proteins, based on the aggregation of AuNPs, distance-dependent phenomena and color change of AuNPs and FRET by enlarging the inter-particle distance between AuNPs and CNCs and recovering the fluorescence emission of CNC. While most of gluten detection methods are immunoassays and based on genomic or antibody detection, this nanosensor is developed from fluorescent gold nanocomposites and is easy to use and requires no special skills or instruments. The proposed method allows the limit of detection concentration of 4.43 ngmL−1 to achieve with the naked eye and 3.13 ngmL−1 for fluorescence within 1 min, which are comparable to immunological methods. More importantly, the nanosensor could not only detect gluten in the 55 % ethanol standard solution but also has sensitive responses toward gluten in real sample media with high sensitivity. These results confirm its potential in bioanalysis, which could be important for patients with celiac disease.

Fatemeh Sadat Mousavizadeh: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Nahid Sarlak: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Lorestan University high education Research Council for financial support of this research.
Ethical Approval This article does not contain any studies with human or animal subjects.

Lange, C, Balny, Reversible changes of the wheat g46 gliadin conformation submitted to high pressures and temperatures, Eur. J. Biochem. 268 (2001) 5705–5712. https://doi.org/10.1046/j.1432-1327.2000.01821.x

sb:title.sb:maintitle: SPR nanosensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer film with gold nanoparticles for sensitive detection of aflatoxin B1
